codeQuality.notes: Well-structured implementation with separate files per runner and clear separation of parsing/formatting. Each filter follows the same pattern (strip → parse → format) making the code predictable. Minor deduction: the strip() ANSI helper is duplicated across all 4 filter files instead of reusing stripAnsi() from src/utils.ts. The implementation correctly splits into 4 files (test-js.ts, test-py.ts, test-rs.ts, test-go.ts) rather than the single test.ts mentioned in IMPLEMENTATION.md, which is a better design choice for maintainability.

testCoverage.notes: Excellent coverage with 56 tests across all 4 runners. Tests cover: command matching (positive and negative), all-pass single-line output, failure detail extraction, stack trace stripping, file:line preservation, savings thresholds (>90% for JS, >80% for py/rs/go), large output generators for realistic savings verification, and registry integration. Each runner has dedicated fixtures for both all-pass and failure scenarios.

issues.description: DRY violation: strip() ANSI helper function is duplicated in all 4 filter files (test-js.ts:18, test-py.ts:15, test-rs.ts:15, test-go.ts:15). The project already has stripAnsi() in src/utils.ts that serves the same purpose. However, the local strip() only handles SGR sequences (\x1b\[[0-9;]*m) while utils.stripAnsi also handles OSC 8 hyperlinks, so the local version is slightly less complete but sufficient for test output.

Weak test assertion at test/test-runners.test.ts:329: expect(result.filtered).toContain('13 pass') — this is a substring match that would pass even if the output said '13 passengers'. Should be '13 passed' for precision. The test does pass correctly since the actual output is '13 passed', but the assertion is imprecise.

The bun test parser (parseBunTest) counts individual pass/fail lines AND reads the numeric summary lines ('13 pass', '2 fail'). The summary lines override the individual counts, which is correct behavior, but if bun's output format ever changes to omit summary lines, the individual counting would be used as fallback. This dual-path parsing is reasonable but could benefit from a comment explaining the priority.

The IMPLEMENTATION.md spec says to 'keep only the at line with file:line' in stack traces, but the implementation extracts file:line as metadata appended in parentheses after the error. This is arguably better (more compact) but deviates slightly from the literal spec wording.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The ANSI stripping pattern is duplicated across multiple filter files. AGENTS.md could document a convention to import stripAnsi from src/utils.ts for consistency, especially as more filters are added.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/filters/test-js.ts:18, test-py.ts:15, test-rs.ts:15, test-go.ts:15 all define their own strip() function. Also observed in other filter files like git-status.ts, git-diff.ts. src/utils.ts already exports stripAnsi() with more complete handling.

summary: The test-runner-filters feature is well-implemented with all 4 filter files (test-js.ts, test-py.ts, test-rs.ts, test-go.ts) correctly parsing their respective runner outputs. All-pass produces single summary lines, failures show compacted output with test names, error lines, and file:line references. Stack traces are properly stripped. All 4 filters registered in the filter registry. 56 tests provide thorough coverage including matching, formatting, savings thresholds, and registry integration. The only non-blocking issue is duplicated ANSI strip helpers across files. All tests pass (56/56) and TypeScript typechecks cleanly.
